rulings.Case Title: GABRIELA GEYER et al VS. MERIDIAN MANAGEMENT GROUP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Motion To Compel Deposition Of Gil Dowd (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 2, PLAINTIFF GABRIELA GEYER, TRAVIS GEYER, GEYER, GABRIELA INDIVIDUALLY AND ON BEHALF, GEYER, TRAVIS, INDIVIDUALLY AND ON BEHALF OF ALL Amended Motion To Compel Deposition Of Gil Dowd. (Part 3 of 3). Plaintiffs argue that the meet and confer process was robust and claim "over thirty emails between Plaintiff and Defense [sic]" have been sent and received, "concerning Mr. Dowd's deposition." But these meet and confer efforts appear to have pertained to the other cases, not the instant one. As Plaintiffs explain: "Plaintiffs are seeking sanctions for the many months of delays that preceded Defendants seeking baseless protective order and will agree to withdraw the recently continued motion [the instant Motion] should Defendants agree to compensate Plaintiffs for the time spend on the motion." In other words Plaintiffs are still fuming about the meet and confer efforts that preceded the motion filed in the Palomo case. But meeting and conferring on Palomo is not the same as meet and confer in this action. Plaintiffs now suggest that the discovery efforts between counsel on any of the six cases relates to all of the six cases. Ironically, this was the position that Meridian attempted to facilitate, namely, coordinating discovery among and between the six action. But Plaintiffs uniformly rejected this solution. As they explain, "Plaintiffs' counsel has maintained that all cases against Defendant, including the prior settled cases, sere independent, and that discovery limitations would prejudice the other independent cases and plaintiffs." Thus if the Court views discovery within each of the six cases as individuated and separate, as Plaintiffs argue it should, then it is beholden upon Plaintiffs to offer evidence of meet and confer efforts that are particular to the deposition of Mr. Dowd noticed in this case. With the exception of Meridian offering to provide Mr. Dowd for deposition a month ago, no such evidence has been submitted by Plaintiffs. "Misuses of the discovery process include . . . failing to confer in person by telephone or by letter with an opposing party or attorney in a reasonable and good faith attempt to resolve informally any dispute concerning discovery." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code s 2023.010. The Court finds that Meridian's offer to make Mr. Dowd available for deposition and Plaintiff's refusal to take Mr. Dowd's deposition for no other reason than to perpetuate its right to seek sanctions against Meridian for what Plaintiffs perceive to be a "history of evasive conduct" in other parallel proceedings, to be an abuse of the discovery process and a failure to properly meet and confer. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GABRIELA GEYER et al VS. MERIDIAN MANAGEMENT GROUP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Mtn To Compel Depo Of Gil Dowd
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 2, PLAINTIFF's Amended Motion To Compel Deposition Of Gil Dowd. (Part 1 of 3). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiffs motion to compel is DENIED without prejudice. Plaintiffs may re-notice Gil Dowd's deposition without prejudice. Parties may seek discovery relief related to Gil Dowd's re-noticed deposition without prejudice. Plaintiffs' request for sanctions is DENIED. Meridian's request for sanctions is GRANTED in an amount to be determined. Meridian shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Plaintiffs' counsel represents plaintiffs in six different lawsuits against Defendant Meridian Management Group ("Meridian") related to bedbugs alleged to be found in the Defendant's apartment buildings. In the instant action, Plaintiffs Gabriela Geyer and Travis Geyer (the "Geyers") noticed Meridian CEO Gil Dowd ("Dowd") for deposition and document production to occur on May 30, 2014. Plaintiffs in the related case of Palomo v. Meridian Management Group simultaneously noticed Mr. Dowd for his deposition to occur on May 13, 2014. With overlapping depositions and the threat of more on the way, Meridian sought to coordinate discovery efforts among and between the six actions. The Geyers and the plaintiffs in the related matters resisted these efforts. Meridian brought a Motion for Protective order on May 30, 2014, in the Palomo case in order to squelch duplicative deposition obligations between the various lawsuits. Then, on June 5, 2014, one day before a global mediation involving all six of these cases, the Geyers noticed their Motion to Compel Mr. Dowd's deposition. That Motion was re-noticed and is now the Instant Motion. The deposition notices in the two cases and the motions in the two cases appear to be substantially similar. Although it is clearly proper to take Mr. Dowd's deposition in this matter, multiple reasons require the Court to deny the motion to compel. Principally the problem with Plaintiffs' approach here is that they do not appear to be using the Discovery Act in order to conduct discovery. Plaintiffs seem to be seeking to use the Discovery Act-at least as of late-as a mechanism to generate monetary returns in the form of sanctions. Sanctions are sometimes appropriate when the opposing party complies with discovery demands only after the filing of a motion. But for reasons discussed below, sanctions are not appropriate to punish Meridian here. (Continued to Part 2 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GABRIELA GEYER et al VS. MERIDIAN MANAGEMENT GROUP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Motion To Compel Deposition Of Gil Dowd (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 2, PLAINTIFF GABRIELA GEYER, TRAVIS GEYER, GEYER, GABRIELA INDIVIDUALLY AND ON BEHALF, GEYER, TRAVIS, INDIVIDUALLY AND ON BEHALF OF ALL Amended Motion To Compel Deposition Of Gil Dowd. (Part 2 of 3). The first difficulty with Plaintiffs Motion is that Mr. Dowd's deposition was noticed by the Geyers. The instant Motion to Compel was also filed by the Geyers. But the Geyers have dismissed their claims. They are no longer part of this action. It is not clear that a deposition noticed by non-existent party, as a predicate event for a motion bought by the same non-existent party may be used to grant relief for the remaining class members. Plaintiffs' counsel argues that "Defendant fails to cite any legal authority . . . that states that class action discovery cannot continue under an unnamed class representative." But Plaintiffs fail to cite any authority either. And given that it is the Plaintiffs' motion, the burden first falls upon them to establish good cause. See Cal. Civ. Proc. Code s 2025.420. Parties are encouraged to research this question and at the hearing, provide to the Court authority as to whether the class members in the instant action do or do not continue to enjoy the effects and benefits of a deposition notice and/or a motion brought by a now non-existent class representative. Second, Plaintiffs have not adequately met and conferred. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2025.450(b)(2). On June 10, 2014 in the wake of a successful mediation that resulted in settling the vast majority of the related actions, the Parties agreed to take the Motion for Protective Order in Palomo and the Motion to Compel in the instant matter, off calendar. Plaintiffs memorialized this agreement in a letter of the same date, cc-ed to Judge Quidachay. And while Meridian did take its Motion for Protective Order in Palomo off calendar, Plaintiffs simply re-noticed the instant Motion six days later. There is no evidence of the Parties meeting and conferring in the intervening six days or what would otherwise justify the Plaintiffs' breach of the stipulation. Third, what meet-and-confer efforts were made after the June 6, 2014 settlement involved Meridian offering to produce Mr. Dowd for deposition on July 9, 2014. Plaintiffs argue that Mr. Dowd was only offered for his deposition by Meridian on the "condition that Mr. Dowd be deposed only once for all cases being handled by the Law Offices of Eric L. Lifschitz." But while that may have been a condition in the past, no evidence of that condition precedent can be found in Meridian's June 17 and 18, 2014 meet and confer communications. Fourth, it appears that Meridian's offer was rejected by Plaintiffs not because to do so would abrogate certain discovery rights but rather, exclusively to allow them to seek sanctions. Plaintiffs even clarify on June 18, 2014 that they are no longer interested in deposing Dowd, at least not for the time being: "Plaintiffs are not seeking to depose Mr. Dowd at this time." Plaintiffs are now using the Discovery Act simply as a tool to collect sanctions. Mr. Dowd's deposition appears to be but a tertiary concern. (Continued to Part 3 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement

rulings.Case Title: SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT VS. PACFIC GAS & ELECRIC COMPANY et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 8: DEFENDANT PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Judge Miller discloses and will disclose at the hearing that she is a member of McFetridge Inn of Court and that Laurie Edelstein, counsel for defendant/cross-defendant PG&E is also a member of the same Inn of Court. This will not affect Judge Miller's ability to be fair and impartial in this case and she does not believe that a person aware of this fact would reasonably entertain a doubt that she would be able to be impartial. Overruled in part and sustained without leave to amend in part. As to the quantum meruit cause of action, the demurrer is overruled. Liberally construing the Second Amended Complaint ("SAC"), the SAC adequately alleges an implied request and benefit. (SAC, pars. 70-71.) As to the account stated cause of action, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. The SAC does not plead a previous relationship between the parties. (Zinn v. Fred R. Bright Co. (1969) 271 Cal.App.2d 597, 600; see also 1 Witkin, Summary 10th (2005) Contacts, sec. 972, p. 1062, stating that an account stated is where "[p]arties transacting business with each other, and keeping accounts of their transaction and items of indebtedness¿come to an agreement on the amount of the final balance due from one another.") Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT VS. PACFIC GAS & ELECRIC COMPANY et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 8: CROSS DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. Judge Miller discloses and will disclose at the hearing that she is a member of McFetridge Inn of Court and that Laurie Edelstein, counsel for defendant/cross-defendant PG&E is also a member of the same Inn of Court. This will not affect Judge Miller's ability to be fair and impartial in this case and she does not believe that a person aware of this fact would reasonably entertain a doubt that she would be able to be impartial. Sustained with 20 days leave to amend. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) As to the 1st cause of action, the plain language of the Franchise Agreements, attached as exhibits to the Cross-Complaint, require the utility company to "remove or relocate" facilities as necessary to accommodate construction projects. (Cross-Complaint, Ex. A, p. A-5, Ex. B, p. B-3.) There is no contractual obligation that the utility company pay for any work around or support of their facilities. Therefore, the Cross-Complaint does not adequately allege breach. As to the 2nd cause of action, the 1974 SWAP Agreement, attached as an exhibit to the Cross-Complaint, explicitly states that it is between "the Pacific Telephone and Telegraph Company¿and the City and County of San Francisco." (Cross-Complaint, Ex. E, p. E-1.) As such, the Cross-Complaint does not adequately plead that PG&E is a party to the Agreement. The 1970 SWAP Agreement, attached as an exhibit to the Cross-Complaint, does not contain a provision requiring the utility company to make payment directly to a private contractor. (Cross-Complaint, Ex. D.) Therefore, the Cross-Complaint does not adequately allege breach. As to the 3rd cause of action, the Cross-Complaint does not allege facts establishing that Sections 906-910 were violated. As to the fifth cause of action, the 1970 SWAP Agreement provides that the utility company must indemnify the City as to claims "arising directly or indirectly from defects in Contract Utility Drawings incorporated in any City contract." (Cross-Complaint, Ex. D, p. D-12.) The Cross-Complaint does not allege damages from defects in Contract Utility Drawings. As to the 7th cause of action, the Cross-Complaint alleges no facts establishing that Cross-Defendant has an obligation to pay Synergy directly under any contract or statute. Therefore, facts are not sufficiently pled to support a declaratory relief cause of action. Cross-Complainant's request for judicial notice is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT VS. PACFIC GAS & ELECRIC COMPANY et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 8: CROSS DEFENDANT PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. Judge Miller discloses and will disclose at the hearing that she is a member of McFetridge Inn of Court and that Laurie Edelstein, counsel for defendant/cross-defendant PG&E is also a member of the same Inn of Court. This will not affect Judge Miller's ability to be fair and impartial in this case and she does not believe that a person aware of this fact would reasonably entertain a doubt that she would be able to be impartial. Sustained with 20 days leave to amend. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) The 1970 and 1974 SWAP Agreements, attached to the Cross-Complaint as exhibits, provide that the indemnification provisions only apply to claims "arising directly or indirectly from defects in Contract Utility Drawings incorporated in any City contract" and for "any loss or damaged sustained by the City by reason of any delays¿" (Cross-Complaint, Ex. D, p. D-12; Ex. E, p. E-4.) The Cross-Complaint does not allege damages from defects in Contract Utility Drawings or damages caused by delay. rulings.Case Title: HORACIO SANCHEZ GOMEZ et al VS. ROBERT DOLAN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Stay Of Discovery And Or A Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, August 5, 2014, Line 1, DEFENDANT ROBERT DOLAN Motion For A Stay Of Discovery And Or A Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant Robert Dolan's Motion for a Stay of Discovery and/or a Protective Order is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. All Parties' requests for sanctions is DENIED. On the one hand, the Court is not inclined to grant a stay on discovery, especially considering the potentiality of an upcoming motion for summary adjudication-a motion that itself mandates the submission of facts and encourages the parties to take discovery while the motion is pending. But the Special Interrogatories and Request for Production of Documents propounded by Plaintiff Horacio Gomez ("Gomez") are undoubtedly excessive. Gomez's counsel's declaration in excess of the Rule of 35 under Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.030, is not facially persuasive. The parties are ordered to meet and confer in advance of the hearing. The parties are ordered to appear at hearing and report on the meet and confer efforts. Each party shall appear at hearing with a proposed order or in the alternative to stipulate to a single proposed order Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
